twitter.hook: Ashwagandha and lemon balm get equal billing on most 'natural anxiety remedies' lists. One has 7+ RCTs and provisional WFSBP clinical guidelines. The other has one small positive trial with 18 participants. This is what the honest evidence ranking looks like.
thread: The framework: three criteria for every intervention. (1) How many independent RCTs? (2) What's the standardized effect size — Hedges' g, Cohen's d, or percent HAM-A reduction? (3) What's the side-effect penalty? Single positive studies don't move anything into Tier A.

Tier 1 — Ashwagandha (KSM-66, 300-600mg/day): Chandrasekhar et al. (Indian J Psych Med, 2012) — 27.9% cortisol reduction vs. placebo. 7+ RCTs. Provisionally included in WFSBP and CANMAT clinical guidelines for generalized anxiety disorder. That's the threshold where clinicians take it seriously.

Tier 1 — Lavender oil Silexan (80-160mg, oral capsule): Kasper et al. (Int J Neuropsychopharmacology, 2014) — HAM-A reduction 14.1 points vs. 9.5 placebo, n=539. 60.3% responder rate vs. 37.8% placebo. Non-inferior to lorazepam in head-to-head trial. No dependency risk. No GABA-A binding.

The lavender caveat nobody mentions: Silexan is a specific standardized extract manufactured by Schwabe Pharmaceuticals. Lavender oil diffused in a room, lavender candles, topical lavender — none of those have this evidence base. They are not the same thing.

L-theanine (200-400mg): Williams et al. (Plant Foods Human Nutrition, 2019) systematic review — 9 RCTs of pure L-theanine, significant reduction in stress and anxiety in acute stress paradigms. The chronic GAD evidence base is thinner. Effect strongest when the stressor is present.

CBD: Skelton et al. (Psychiatry Research, 2024) meta-analysis — 8 RCTs, Hedges' g = -0.92. That looks impressive until you read the fine print: 70.3% of observations in the broader CBD literature show no effect on anxiety outcomes. Most qualifying trials used a single acute dose. High risk of bias throughout.

Kava: 12 RCTs, real efficacy, kavalactones modulate GABA-A. But the NCCIH warning is unambiguous — rare cases of liver failure requiring transplantation and at least one death. Germany, Canada, Switzerland, UK have all issued regulatory restrictions. The evidence for efficacy is real. The risk-benefit calculation is unresolved.

What performs better than most supplements on the list: Hofmann et al. 2010 — 39 mindfulness studies, 1,140 participants, moderate effect on anxiety. Stubbs et al. 2017 — 6 RCTs, 262 adults, SMD = -0.58. Exercise and mindfulness outperform most of the supplement list. Neither appears on most 'natural remedies' rankings.

Lemon balm at 150-300mg: one small positive trial with 18 participants, single dose, stress model. It shares bullet points with ashwagandha on most lists. The gap between those two evidence bases is the entire problem with this category.
twitter.cta: 18 interventions ranked by RCT count, effect size, and side-effect profile: https://www.blackswanp.com/research/mental-work/anxiety-natural-remedies — Share if someone in your life is treating lemon balm and ashwagandha as equivalent.

linkedin.hook:
Most 'natural anxiety remedies' lists rank interventions by how popular they are, not by what the evidence shows.

Lavender oil (Silexan, specific oral extract): non-inferior to lorazepam in a head-to-head RCT, 539 participants, no dependency risk.

Lemon balm: one trial with 18 participants.

They share bullet points on most lists. They should not.
linkedin.body:
The anxiety supplement category has a systematic credibility problem: the same lists rank interventions with 7+ replicated RCTs alongside interventions with a single pilot trial, as if the evidence behind each were comparable.

It is not. Here's what the honest ranking looks like.

Tier 1 — Replicated RCT evidence, real effect sizes:

Ashwagandha (KSM-66, 300-600mg/day): Chandrasekhar et al. (Indian J Psych Med, 2012): 27.9% cortisol reduction vs. placebo, n=64. Seven or more independent RCTs. Provisionally included in WFSBP and CANMAT clinical guidelines for GAD — the threshold where practicing clinicians take a supplement seriously.

Silexan lavender oil (80-160mg oral capsule): Kasper et al. (Int J Neuropsychopharmacology, 2014): HAM-A reduction of 14.1 points vs. 9.5 placebo, n=539. 60.3% responder rate vs. 37.8% placebo. Non-inferior to lorazepam in a separate head-to-head trial. Mechanism: voltage-dependent calcium channels, not GABA-A — no dependency, no rebound, no abuse potential. Note: Silexan is a specific proprietary extract from Schwabe Pharmaceuticals. Lavender aromatherapy is not this intervention.

L-theanine (200-400mg): nine RCTs of pure L-theanine, significant acute stress reduction. Williams et al. (Plant Foods Human Nutrition, 2019). Effect strongest in acute-stress paradigms; chronic GAD evidence base is smaller.

Tier 2 — Real but smaller evidence base:

Saffron (30mg standardized): Hedges' g = 0.95 vs. placebo in a 2019 meta-analysis for anxiety — large effect, but smaller total database and most trials are short-duration.

Passionflower: two Cochrane-eligible RCTs total. Cochrane conclusion: insufficient evidence for definitive conclusions.

CBD: Skelton et al. (Psychiatry Research, 2024) — Hedges' g = -0.92, but 70.3% of broader literature shows no effect, mostly single-dose trials, high risk of bias.

What outperforms most of the supplement list and doesn't appear on it: exercise (SMD = -0.58, Stubbs et al. 2017, 6 RCTs) and mindfulness (moderate effect, Hofmann et al. 2010, 39 studies). Both have deeper evidence than anything in Tier 2.

Full ranked breakdown with primary citations: https://www.blackswanp.com/research/mental-work/anxiety-natural-remedies

slides.title: SLIDE 1 — HOOK
slides.body: Lavender oil: non-inferior to lorazepam in a 539-person RCT. No dependency risk. Lemon balm: one 18-person trial. They share a bullet on most lists. They should not.

slides.title: SLIDE 2 — STAT
slides.body: Kasper et al., Int J Neuropsychopharmacology 2014 (n=539, Silexan): HAM-A reduction 14.1 points vs. 9.5 placebo. 60.3% responder rate vs. 37.8%. Non-inferior to lorazepam. No GABA binding. No dependency.

slides.title: SLIDE 3 — REVEAL
slides.body: CBD meta-analysis 2024 (Psychiatry Research, 8 RCTs): Hedges' g = -0.92. Looks large. But: 70.3% of the broader literature shows no effect. Most qualifying trials used a single dose. High risk of bias.

slides.title: SLIDE 4 — DETAIL
slides.body: Ashwagandha (KSM-66) is the only herbal anxiolytic provisionally included in WFSBP and CANMAT clinical guidelines for GAD — the threshold where working clinicians take a supplement seriously.

slides.title: SLIDE 5 — QUOTE
slides.body: Exercise (SMD = -0.58, Stubbs 2017, 6 RCTs) and mindfulness (Hofmann 2010, 39 studies) outperform most of the supplement list — and appear on almost none of them.

slides.title: SLIDE 6 — IMPLICATION
slides.body: Kava: real efficacy (12 RCTs), real liver risk (at least one transplant, one death). Germany, Canada, Switzerland, UK all issued restrictions. Evidence for efficacy: real. Risk-benefit: unresolved.

slides.title: SLIDE 7 — SOURCES
slides.body: Chandrasekhar et al., Indian J Psych Med (2012) · Kasper et al., Int J Neuropsychopharmacology (2014) · Skelton et al., Psychiatry Research (2024) · Stubbs et al., Psychiatry Research (2017)

slides.title: SLIDE 8 — CTA
slides.body: 18 interventions ranked by actual evidence at blackswanp.com/research/mental-work/anxiety-natural-remedies

reddit.title: Natural anxiety interventions ranked by RCT evidence and effect size — what actually makes the Tier A cut
reddit.body:
I went through the primary literature to rank 18 commonly recommended anxiety interventions by three criteria: number of independent RCTs, standardized effect size, and side-effect profile. Here's what makes the cut and what doesn't.

TIER A (multiple RCTs, real effect sizes):

Ashwagandha (KSM-66): Chandrasekhar 2012 (n=64, 60 days) — 27.9% cortisol reduction vs. placebo. 7+ independent RCTs. WFSBP and CANMAT have provisionally included KSM-66 in clinical guidelines for GAD.

Silexan lavender oil (oral, 80-160mg): Kasper 2014 (n=539) — HAM-A 14.1 vs. 9.5 placebo. Non-inferior to lorazepam in 2010 trial. No dependency (calcium channel mechanism, not GABA-A). Note: this is a specific Schwabe Pharmaceuticals extract. Not lavender aromatherapy.

L-theanine (200-400mg): Williams 2019 systematic review, 9 RCTs, significant acute stress reduction. Chronic GAD evidence base smaller.

Saffron (30mg standardized): Hedges' g = 0.95 in 2019 meta-analysis. Smaller database, short trials. Avoid in pregnancy at high doses.

TIER B (real but thin evidence):

Magnesium (200-400mg elemental): Boyle 2017 systematic review — 'suggestive' evidence in deficient populations. Effect concentrates where dietary intake is below RDA.

Passionflower: 2 Cochrane-eligible RCTs. Cochrane: 'insufficient evidence for definitive conclusions.'

CBD: g = -0.92 in Skelton 2024 meta-analysis, but 70.3% of broader literature shows no effect. Mostly single-dose trials. High bias.

NOT EVIDENCE-BASED:
Lemon balm at supplement doses (150-300mg): one small positive trial, 18 participants, single-dose model. Holy basil: animal studies + one open-label human trial. Most 'calm blend' products: zero qualifying RCTs.

OUTPERFORMS MOST OF THE LIST AND DOESN'T APPEAR ON IT:
Exercise (Stubbs 2017: SMD = -0.58, 6 RCTs, 262 adults) and mindfulness (Hofmann 2010: moderate effect, 39 studies, 1,140 participants).

Full citation breakdown: https://www.blackswanp.com/research/mental-work/anxiety-natural-remedies

reddit.title: Silexan lavender (oral capsule) was non-inferior to lorazepam in a head-to-head trial — why isn't this more widely known?
reddit.body:
I keep seeing 'lavender oil' listed as a mild, anecdotal supplement for anxiety, but the actual clinical data for the specific oral extract is a lot stronger than that framing suggests.

Silexan is a standardized lavender oil extract manufactured by Schwabe Pharmaceuticals. It's taken as an 80-160mg oral capsule — not aromatherapy, not topical, not diffuser. The mechanism is distinct from benzodiazepines: it acts on voltage-dependent calcium channels, not GABA-A receptors. No dependency. No rebound anxiety. No abuse potential.

The evidence:

Kasper et al. 2010 (Phytomedicine): direct head-to-head with lorazepam (0.5mg/day), n=77. Silexan 80mg was non-inferior on HAM-A reduction. This is a comparison to a pharmaceutical anxiolytic.

Kasper et al. 2014 (Int J Neuropsychopharmacology): largest Silexan trial — n=539, 10 weeks, GAD diagnosis. HAM-A reduction: 14.1 points (Silexan 160mg) vs. 9.5 placebo. Responder rate: 60.3% vs. 37.8%. The 80mg dose also outperformed placebo significantly.

For context: the WFSBP and CANMAT clinical guidelines haven't yet formally included lavender, but ashwagandha (which has a similar evidence tier) has been provisionally included. The trial database for Silexan is better-designed than most herbal anxiolytics.

The caveat: Silexan is not generic lavender oil capsules. Product quality matters. The trials used the specific Schwabe extract.

For a full ranking of natural anxiety interventions by evidence: https://www.blackswanp.com/research/mental-work/anxiety-natural-remedies

(Always consult a physician before combining supplements with prescribed anxiolytics.)

email.subject: The honest natural anxiety evidence ranking
email.preheader: Lavender oil beat lorazepam in a 539-person RCT. Lemon balm has 18 participants. Both are on most lists.
email.body:
Most 'natural anxiety remedies' lists treat ashwagandha and lemon balm as equivalent. One has 7+ replicated RCTs and provisional WFSBP clinical guidelines. The other has one trial with 18 participants.

The honest Tier A: ashwagandha (KSM-66, 27.9% cortisol reduction, WFSBP-provisioned), Silexan lavender oil (non-inferior to lorazepam in a 539-person RCT, no dependency), magnesium in deficient populations, and L-theanine in acute stress paradigms.

What outperforms most of the supplement list and doesn't appear on it: exercise (SMD = -0.58 across 6 RCTs) and mindfulness (moderate effect across 39 studies).

What earns real efficacy points but carries real liver risk: kava. Germany, Canada, Switzerland, and the UK have all issued regulatory restrictions.

Full 18-intervention ranked file: https://www.blackswanp.com/research/mental-work/anxiety-natural-remedies

Subscribe for one transmission per week.

pullQuotes: Silexan lavender oil was non-inferior to lorazepam — no dependency, no rebound.
70.3% of CBD anxiety studies show no effect. The g=-0.92 headline hides that.
Exercise outperforms most of the supplement list and appears on almost none of them.
WFSBP provisionally included ashwagandha in GAD guidelines. That's the clinical threshold.
Lemon balm: one trial, 18 people. Ashwagandha: 7+ RCTs. Same bullet on most lists.
